quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Nada, Lucílio, é nosso, exceto o tempo.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra a edição de Gummere (Letter 1).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Estar em toda parte é não estar em lugar nenhum.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra a edição de Gummere (Letter 2).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: You have sent a letter to me through the hand of a “friend” of yours, as you call him.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Keep on as you have begun, and make all possible haste, so that you may have longer enjoyment of an improved mind, one that is at peace with itself.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I commend you and rejoice in the fact that you are persistent in your studies, and that, putting all else aside, you make it each day your endeavour to become a better man.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I feel, my dear Lucilius, that I am being not only reformed, but transformed.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Do you ask me what you should regard as especially to be avoided?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: “Do you bid me,” you say, “shun the throng, and withdraw from men, and be content with my own conscience?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: O sábio é autossuficiente; ainda assim, deseja amigos.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra a edição de Gummere (Letter 9).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Yes, I do not change my opinion: avoid the many, avoid the few, avoid even the individual.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Your friend and I have had a conversation. He is a man of ability; his very first words showed what spirit and understanding he possesses, and what progress he has already made.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Wherever I turn, I see evidences of my advancing years. I visited lately my country-place, and protested against the money which was spent on the tumble-down building.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I know that you have plenty of spirit; for even before you began to equip yourself with maxims which were wholesome and potent to overcome obstacles, you were taking pride in your contest with Fortune; and this is all the more true, now that you have grappled with Fortune and tested your powers.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I confess that we all have an inborn affection for our body; I confess that we are entrusted with its guardianship.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: The old Romans had a custom which survived even into my lifetime.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: It is clear to you, I am sure, Lucilius, that no man can live a happy life, or even a supportable life, without the study of wisdom; you know also that a happy life is reached when our wisdom is brought to completion, but that life is at least endurable even when our wisdom is only begun.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Cast away everything of that sort, if you are wise; nay, rather that you may be wise; strive toward a sound mind at top speed and with your whole strength.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: It is the month of December, and yet the city is at this very moment in a sweat.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I leap for joy whenever I receive letters from you. For they fill me with hope; they are now not mere assurances concerning you, but guarantees.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: If you are in good health and if you think yourself worthy of becoming at last your own master, I am glad.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Do you conclude that you are having difficulties with those men about whom you wrote to me?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: You understand by this time that you must withdraw yourself from those showy and depraved pursuits; but you still wish to know how this may be accomplished.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Do you suppose that I shall write you how kindly the winter season has dealt with us,—a short season and a mild one,—or what a nasty spring we are having,—cold weather out of season,—and all the other trivialities which people write when they are at a loss for topics of conversation?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: You write me that you are anxious about the result of a lawsuit, with which an angry opponent is threatening you; and you expect me to advise you to picture to yourself a happier issue, and to rest in the allurements of hope.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: With regard to these two friends of ours, we must proceed along different lines; the faults of the one are to be corrected, the other’s are to be crushed out.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I was just lately telling you that I was within sight of old age.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: “What,” say you, “are you giving me advice? Indeed, have you already advised yourself, already corrected your own faults?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: Do you suppose that you alone have had this experience? Are you surprised, as if it were a novelty, that after such long travel and so many changes of scene you have not been able to shake off the gloom and heaviness of your mind?
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: You have been inquiring about our friend Marcellinus and you desire to know how he is getting along.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: seneca
quotes.quote_text: I have beheld Aufidius Bassus, that noble man, shattered in health and wrestling with his years.
quotes.source_edition: Richard M. Gummere (Loeb Classical Library, 1918/1925), via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Algumas coisas dependem de nós; outras não.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra The Encheiridion or Manual, seção I.

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Remember that desire contains in it the profession (hope) of obtaining that which you desire; and the profession (hope) in aversion (turning from a thing) is that you will not fall into that which you attempt to avoid: and he who fails in his desire is unfortunate; and he who falls into that which he would avoid, is unhappy.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: In every thing which pleases the soul, or supplies a want, or is loved, remember to add this to the (description, notion); what is the nature of each thing, beginning from the smallest?
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: When you are going to take in hand any act, remind yourself what kind of an act it is.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Os homens não se perturbam pelas coisas que acontecem, mas pelas opiniões sobre elas.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra The Encheiridion or Manual, seção V.

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Be not elated at any advantage (excellence), which belongs to another.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: As on a voyage when the vessel has reached a port, if you go out to get water, it is an amusement by the way to pick up a shell fish or some bulb, but your thoughts ought to be directed to the ship, and you ought to be constantly watching if the captain should call, and then you must throw away all those things, that you may not be bound and pitched into the ship like sheep: so in life also, if there be given to you instead of a little bulb and a shell a wife and child, there will be nothing to prevent (you from taking them).
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are, and you will have a tranquil flow of life.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Disease is an impediment to the body, but not to the will, unless the will itself chooses.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: On the occasion of every accident (event) that befals you, remember to turn to yourself and inquire what power you have for turning it to use.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Never say about any thing, I have lost it, but say I have restored it.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If you intend to improve, throw away such thoughts as these: if I neglect my affairs, I shall not have the means of living: unless I chastise my slave, he will be bad.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If you would improve, submit to be considered without sense and foolish with respect to externals.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If you would have your children and your wife and your friends to live for ever, you are silly; for you would have the things which are not in your power to be in your power, and the things which belong to others to be yours.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Remember that in life you ought to behave as at a banquet. Suppose that something is carried round and is opposite to you.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: When you see a person weeping in sorrow either when a child goes abroad or when he is dead, or when the man has lost his property, take care that the appearance do not hurry you away with it, as if he were suffering in external things.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Remember that thou art an actor in a play, of such a kind as the teacher (author) may choose; if short, of a short one; if long, of a long one: if he wishes you to act the part of a poor man, see that you act the part naturally; if the part of a lame man, of a magistrate, of a private person, (do the same).
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: When a raven has croaked inauspiciously, let not the appearance hurry you away with it; but straightway make a distinction in your mind and say, None of these things is signified to me, but either to my poor body, or to my small property, or to my reputation, or to my children or to my wife: but to me all significations are auspicious if I choose.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: You can be invincible, if you enter into no contest in which it is not in your power to conquer.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Lembre-se: não é quem o insulta ou fere que o ofende, mas a sua opinião de que isso é insulto.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra The Encheiridion or Manual, seção XX.

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Let death and exile and every other thing which appears dreadful be daily before your eyes; but most of all death: and you will never think of any thing mean nor will you desire any thing extravagantly.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If you desire philosophy, prepare yourself from the beginning to be ridiculed, to expect that many will sneer at you, and say, He has all at once returned to us as a philosopher; and whence does he get this supercilious look for us?
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If it should ever happen to you to be turned to externals in order to please some person, you must know that you have lost your purpose in life.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Let not these thoughts afflict you, I shall live unhonoured and be nobody nowhere.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Has any man been preferred before you at a banquet, or in being saluted, or in being invited to a consultation?
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: We may learn the wish (will) of nature from the things in which we do not differ from one another: for instance, when your neighbour's slave has broken his cup, or any thing else, we are ready to say forthwith, that it is one of the things which happen.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: As a mark is not set up for the purpose of missing the aim, so neither does the nature of evil exist in the world.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: If any person was intending to put your body in the power of any man whom you fell in with on the way, you would be vexed: but that you put your understanding in the power of any man whom you meet, so that if he should revile you, it is disturbed and troubled, are you not ashamed at this?.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: In every act observe the things which come first, and those which follow it; and so proceed to the act.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: epictetus
quotes.quote_text: Duties are universally measured by relations ( ταῖς σχέσεσι ).
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1877), The Discourses of Epictetus; with the Encheiridion and Fragments, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Ao começar a manhã, diga a si mesmo: encontrarei o intrometido, o ingrato, o arrogante, o enganador, o invejoso e o insociável.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra Book II, seção 1.

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Em nenhum lugar o homem se recolhe com mais tranquilidade e liberdade de perturbação do que na própria alma.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra Book IV, seção 3.

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Se algo é difícil para ti, não penses que é impossível ao homem; se é possível ao homem e conforme à sua natureza, considera que também está ao teu alcance.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra Book VI, seção 19.

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: A mente assume o caráter dos pensamentos habituais; a alma é tingida por eles.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Verificado contra Book V, seção 16.

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Every moment think steadily as a Roman and a man to do what thou hast in hand with perfect and simple dignity, and feeling of affection, and freedom, and justice, and to give thyself relief from all other thoughts.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Do wrong to thyself, do wrong to thyself, my soul; but thou wilt no longer have the opportunity of honoring thyself.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Do the things external which fall upon thee distract thee? Give thyself time to learn something new and good, and cease to be whirled around.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Through not observing what is in the mind of another a man has seldom been seen to be unhappy; but those who do not observe the movements of their own minds must of necessity be unhappy.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: This thou must always bear in mind, what is the nature of the whole, and what is my nature, and how this is related to that, and what kind of a part it is of what kind of a whole, and that there is no one who hinders thee from always doing and saying the things which are according to the nature of which thou art a part.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Theophrastus, in his comparison of bad acts—such a comparison as one would make in accordance with the common notions of mankind—says, like a true philosopher, that the offenses which are committed through desire are more blamable than those which are committed through anger.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Since it is possible that thou mayest depart from life this very moment, regulate every act and thought accordingly.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: How quickly all things disappear,—in the universe the bodies themselves, but in time the remembrance of them.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Nothing is more wretched than a man who traverses everything in a round, and pries into the things beneath the earth, as the poet says, and seeks by conjecture what is in the minds of his neighbors, without perceiving that it is sufficient to attend to the daemon within him, and to reverence it sincerely.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Though thou shouldest be going to live three thousand years and as many times ten thousand years, still remember that no man loses any other life than this which he now lives, nor lives any other than this which he now loses.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Remember that all is opinion. For what was said by the Cynic Monimus is manifest: and manifest too is the use of what was said, if a man receives what may be got out of it as far as it is true.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: The soul of man does violence to itself, first of all, when it becomes an abscess, and, as it were, a tumor on the universe, so far as it can.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Of human life the time is a point, and the substance is in a flux, and the perception dull, and the composition of the whole body subject to putrefaction, and the soul a whirl, and fortune hard to divine, and fame a thing devoid of judgment.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: WE ought to consider not only that our life is daily wasting away and a smaller part of it is left, but another thing also must be taken into the account, that if a man should live longer, it is quite uncertain whether the understanding will still continue sufficient for the comprehension of things, and retain the power of contemplation which strives to acquire the knowledge of the divine and the human.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: We ought to observe also that even the things which follow after the things which are produced according to nature contain something pleasing and attractive.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Hippocrates, after curing many diseases, himself fell sick and died.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Do not waste the remainder of thy life in thoughts about others, when thou dost not refer thy thoughts to some object of common utility.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Labor not unwillingly, nor without regard to the common interest, nor without due consideration, nor with distraction; nor let studied ornament set off thy thoughts, and be not either a man of many words, or busy about too many things.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: If thou findest in human life anything better than justice, truth, temperance, fortitude, and, in a word, anything better than thy own mind's self-satisfaction in the things which it enables thee to do according to right reason, and in the condition that is assigned to thee without thy own choice; if, I say, thou seest anything better than this, turn to it with all thy soul, and enjoy that which thou hast found to be the best.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Never value anything as profitable to thyself which shall compel thee to break thy promise, to lose thy self-respect, to hate any man, to suspect, to curse, to act the hypocrite, to desire anything which needs walls and curtains: for he who has preferred to everything else his own intelligence and daemon and the worship of its excellence, acts no tragic part, does not groan, will not need either solitude or much company; and, what is chief of all, he will live without either pursuing or flying from [death]; but whether for a longer or a shorter time he shall have the soul enclosed in the body, he cares not at all: for even if he must depart immediately, he will go as readily as if he were going to do anything else which can be done with decency and order; taking care of this only all through life, that his thoughts turn not away from anything which belongs to an intelligent animal and a member of a civil community.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: In the mind of one who is chastened and purified thou wilt find no corrupt matter, nor impurity, nor any sore skinned over.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Reverence the faculty which produces opinion. On this faculty it entirely depends whether there shall exist in thy ruling part any opinion inconsistent with nature and the constitution of the rational animal.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: Throwing away then all things, hold to these only which are few; and besides, bear in mind that every man lives only this present time, which is an indivisible point, and that all the rest of his life is either past or it is uncertain.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: To the aids which have been mentioned let this one still be added: Make for thyself a definition or description of the thing which is presented to thee, so as to see distinctly what kind of a thing it is in its substance, in its nudity, in its complete entirety, and tell thyself its proper name, and the names of the things of which it has been compounded, and into which it will be resolved.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: If thou workest at that which is before thee, following right reason seriously, vigorously, calmly, without allowing anything else to distract thee, but keeping thy divine part pure, as if thou shouldst be bound to give it back immediately; if thou holdest to this, expecting nothing, fearing nothing, but satisfied with thy present activity according to nature, and with heroic truth in every word and sound which thou utterest, thou wilt live happy.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).

quotes.author: marcus_aurelius
quotes.quote_text: As physicians have always their instruments and knives ready for cases which suddenly require their skill, so do thou have principles ready for the understanding of things divine and human, and for doing everything, even the smallest, with a recollection of the bond which unites the divine and human to one another.
quotes.source_edition: George Long (1889), The Thoughts of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, via Wikisource
quotes.verification_notes: Extração literal automatizada de fonte canônica; revisão cruzada editorial pendente (SC-04).
